title: Sensitivity to perturbations in the three-dimensional Anderson model
authors: Piotr Tokarczyk, Lev Vidmar, Anatoli Polkovnikov, Patrycja Łydżba
abstract: We investigate the fidelity susceptibility, which quantifies the sensitivity of single-particle eigenstates to perturbations, in the three-dimensional Anderson model. As a function of disorder strength $W$, it exhibits two distinct peaks. The first peak signals a crossover at weak disorder strength from plane-wave states to single-particle quantum chaos, and its position shifts toward $W\to 0$ in the thermodynamic limit. The second peak emerges, to high numerical accuracy, at the critical disorder strength associated with the Anderson localization transition. We further show that the divergence of the first peak is maximal, scaling as the square of the inverse frequency cutoff, whereas the divergence of the second peak is submaximal. We relate the latter suppression to the fractal structure of single-particle eigenstates at criticality. We discuss two distinct scenarios that give rise to the peaks in the fidelity susceptibilities. Moreover, studying the scaling of typical fidelity susceptibilities above the Anderson transition, we find evidence of two distinct regimes of nonergodic behavior.
summary: This paper demonstrates that fidelity susceptibility can identify two distinct transitions in the 3D Anderson model: a crossover to quantum chaos and the Anderson localization transition. It connects the scaling of these susceptibility peaks to the fractal nature of critical eigenstates and identifies distinct nonergodic regimes in the localized phase.
main_problem: The study investigates the sensitivity of single-particle eigenstates to perturbations in the 3D Anderson model, specifically looking for quantitative signatures of the Anderson localization transition and the weak-disorder crossover.
main_result: The fidelity susceptibility exhibits two distinct peaks: one at weak disorder signaling a crossover to quantum chaos, and another at the critical disorder strength associated with Anderson localization. The scaling of these peaks is linked to the fractal dimension of the critical wavefunctions.
method: Numerical simulation using finite-size scaling on cubic lattices up to L=38, employing regularized fidelity susceptibility with a frequency cutoff and spectral analysis of the spectral function.
model_or_system: The 3D Anderson model, a non-interacting single-particle model on a cubic lattice with nearest-neighbor hopping and uncorrelated on-site disorder.
key_observables: Fidelity susceptibility (typical and average), regularized fidelity susceptibility, spectral function, level spacing ratio (r-parameter), and fractal dimension (d2).
important_parameters: Disorder strength (W), frequency cutoff (mu), system size (V), and the critical disorder strength (W2 approx 16.5).
main_assumptions: Assumes the validity of single-particle Eigenstate Thermalization Hypothesis (ETH) in the chaotic regime and assumes the perturbation is a local operator.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the spectral function behavior (Lorentzian broadening vs. power-law decay), the distribution of fidelity susceptibilities (P(chi)), and the scaling of typical vs. average susceptibility.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the fidelity susceptibility as a diagnostic tool, describes the 3D Anderson model, analyzes the weak-disorder crossover and the localization transition, discusses the scaling of peaks in relation to wavefunction fractality, and explores nonergodic regimes in the localized phase.
why_interesting: It provides deep insights into how sensitivity to perturbations can act as a diagnostic for ergodicity-breaking transitions and the emergence of non-ergodic behavior, which is highly relevant to many-body dynamics and the study of localization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) ˜χtyp and (b) ˜χr typ as functions of W. Panel (c) shows the same results as panel (b), but plotted versus W √

FIG. 2. The weak-disorder crossover. (a) The finite-size scal- ing of W ∗ 1 . (b) ˜χr typ evaluated at W ∗ 1 and plotted against µ−1. All results were established from second-order polynomial fits to numerical results from Fig. 1(b). Lines in (a) and (b) are least-squares fits of cx and bxa to V ≥183, respectively. We obtain c ≈41 and a ≈0.98.

FIG. 3. The spectral functions for the 3D Anderson model near (a) the weak-disorder crossover and (b) the Anderson lo- calization transition. Results are obtained from 20% of eigen- states in the middle of the spectrum and averaged over five disorder realizations. The inset in (a) demonstrates the recov- ery of system-size independence when plotting |f(ω)|2/V as a function of ωV . Red curves in panels (a) and (b) show the least-squares fits to the data with V = 383, using the forms C/(ω2 + Γ2) and b ω−a, respectively. We obtain Γ ≈0.0027 and a ≈0.52.

FIG. 4. The Anderson localization transition. Results in (a,b) and (c,d) were established from second-order polynomial fits to numerical results from Figs. 1(a) and 1(b), respectively. (a,c) The finite-size scaling of W ∗ 2 . (b) ˜χtyp evaluated at W ∗ 2 and plotted versus ω−1 typ. (d) ˜χr typ evaluated at W ∗ 2 and plotted versus µ−1. Lines in panels (a,c) and (d) are least- squares fits of dx + c and bxa, respectively. These fits are performed for V ≥183 [with the exception of ˆn in panels (a,b), where V ≥303 is considered]. For ˆTs, ˆT, ˆn, we obtain c = 16.42, 16.45, 16.48 in (a), c = 16.66, 16.76, 17.23 in (c) and a = 0.546, 0.567, 0.554 in (b). Lines in panel (d) are guides to the eye...

FIG. 6. The weak-disorder crossover: (a) the peak position, W ∗ 1 , versus µ1/2, and (b) the peak height, ˜χr typ(W ∗ 1 ), versus µ−1. The Anderson localization transition: (c) the peak po- sition, W ∗ 2 , versus µ1/3 and (d) the peak height, ˜χr typ(W ∗ 2 ), versus µ−1. We consider V = 283, 343 and 383. All results were obtained from second-order polynomial fits to numerical data, analogous to those shown in Fig. 5. The black lines in (a,b) show the least-squares fits of bxa applied to the power- law regions (moderate µ) of the plots for V = 383. The red dashed line in (c) marks the expected W ∗ 2 ≈16.5.
